bibliographic_data.title: ADAM: A Systematic Data Extraction Attack on Agent Memory via Adaptive Querying
bibliographic_data.abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have achieved rapid adoption and demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of applications. To improve reasoning and task execution, modern LLM agents would incorporate memory modules or retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) mechanisms, enabling them to further leverage prior interactions or external knowledge. However, such a design also introduces a group of critical privacy vulnerabilities: sensitive information stored in memory can be leaked through query-based attacks. Although feasible, existing attacks often achieve only limited performance, with low attack success rates (ASR). In this paper, we propose ADAM, a novel privacy attack that features data distribution estimation of a victim agent’s memory and employs an entropy-guided query strategy for maximizing privacy leakage. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our attack substantially outperforms state-of-the-art ones, achieving up to 100% ASRs.

core_content.novelty_claim: ADAM is a novel adaptive data extraction attack that integrates data distribution estimation, active learning, and entropy-based query generation for effectively extracting private records from the victim LLM agent.
core_content.key_methodology: ADAM uses an entropy-guided query strategy to maximize privacy leakage by estimating the underlying data distribution of the victim agent’s memory. It iteratively refines its queries based on observed responses and dynamically adapts to the estimated data distribution.
outcomes: Achieved up to 100% ASRs in extensive experiments
First to identify the importance of data distribution on private data extraction attacks on LLM agents
core_content.full_text_markdown: ADAM is a novel adaptive data extraction attack that integrates data distribution estimation, active learning, and entropy-based query generation for effectively extracting private records from the victim LLM agent. The paper addresses three main limitations of existing methods: reliance on static, manually crafted prompts; focus on stand-alone RAG pipelines; and lack of exploration from the perspective of the underlying data distribution of the victim agent’s memory. ADAM substantially outperforms state-of-the-art attacks in extensive experiments, achieving up to 100% ASRs. The authors also perform extensive evaluations across three real-world agents and their associated datasets, four LLMs, four recent baselines, and four existing defenses, demonstrating that ADAM consistently surpasses the baselines in attack performance.
